---
title: "4.8 ArrayList Methods"
subtitle: "Data Collections"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## ArrayList Methods

### `ArrayList` is used when collection size needs to change

![Dynamic list idea for changing collections](../../assets/4-data-collections/4-8-arraylist-methods/lists.jpg){fig-align="center" width="68%"}

The textbook introduces `ArrayList` as the flexible alternative to fixed-size arrays.

::: {.tight-list}
- arrays have fixed length
- `ArrayList` can grow and shrink during runtime
- it stores object references, not primitive values
:::

## Import and Declaration

### `ArrayList` comes from `java.util`

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;
ArrayList<String> names = new ArrayList<String>();
```

Retained ideas:

::: {.tight-list}
- classes like `String` and `Math` are already available from `java.lang`
- `ArrayList` must be imported from `java.util`
- the type parameter `E` or a concrete type such as `String` identifies what the list stores
- using a typed `ArrayList<E>` is preferred over a raw `ArrayList`
:::

## Creating Lists and `size()`

### Declaring a variable does not create the list object

```java
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<String>();
int n = list.size();
```

::: {.tight-list}
- a new `ArrayList()` starts empty
- `size()` returns the current number of elements
- calling methods on `null` still causes `NullPointerException`
:::

## `add(obj)` and `add(index, obj)`

### Items can be appended or inserted

Retained behaviors:

::: {.tight-list}
- `add(obj)` appends to the end of the list
- `add(index, obj)` inserts at a position
- insertion shifts later elements to the right
- indices start at `0`
:::

Wrapper classes matter here because primitive types are added through autoboxing.

## `remove(index)`

### Removing an element also shifts the remaining elements

```java
E removed = list.remove(index);
```

Key idea:

::: {.tight-list}
- the element at the index is removed and returned
- later elements shift left
- the list becomes smaller by one
:::

## `get(index)` and `set(index, obj)`

### Access and replacement use methods, not square brackets

Because `ArrayList` is a class rather than a built-in array type:

::: {.tight-list}
- `get(index)` reads the element
- `set(index, obj)` replaces the element
- valid indices are `0` through `size() - 1`
- `ArrayList` uses methods instead of `[]`
:::

## Arrays vs `ArrayList`

### The choice depends on whether size must change

The retained comparison is:

::: {.tight-list}
- use an array when the number of items is known and stable
- use `ArrayList` when the size may change
- arrays use `length` and `[]`
- `ArrayList` uses `size()`, `get`, and `set`
:::

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- import and declare typed `ArrayList` variables
- distinguish an empty list from a `null` reference
- use `size()`, `add`, `remove`, `get`, and `set` correctly
- explain why wrapper classes are needed for numeric values
- compare the operations of arrays and `ArrayList`
- <span class="term">4.8.10 Coding Challenge: FRQ Digits</span>
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- explain why `ArrayList` is used instead of an array in dynamic-size situations
- identify the package import needed for `ArrayList`
- explain what the generic type parameter means
- distinguish `add(obj)` from `add(index, obj)`
- explain how `remove`, `get`, `set`, and `size()` work
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
